coredata.dc:title: Scalable one-pot synthesis of porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites for efficient visible-light driven photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Guohui
Wang, Jiangpeng
Wu, Xiuqin
Zhan, Yingying
Liang, Shijing
coredata.dc:description: A porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposite (PCN) has been successfully prepared by a simple and green one-pot photochemical oxidation method for the first time. Notably, the PCN samples are easily one-pot synthesized in hundreds of grams in the laboratory. The structure features, morphologies and photoabsorption performances of the samples are characterized in detail. The photocatalytic activities are evaluated by hydrogen evolution under visible light irradiation. It was proven that the green chemical scissor, produced by the photodecomposition of H2O2, can tailor the texture of layered C3N4 to form porous C3N4 nanosheets and C3N4 quantum dots (QDs) without doping heteroatom merely through the destruction of part of triazine rings, and the as-prepared PCN sample still keeps the basic chemical structure of g-C3N4. The band alignment between CN QDs and C3N4 nanosheets as well as the porous structure will facilitate the separation of charge carriers and provide more active sites. Therefore, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of PCN is over 30 times higher than that of the parent bulk g-C3N4. The activity is also higher than that of ultrathin pore-free C3N4 nanosheets and porous C3N4 nanosheets. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of PCN could be further improved by modifying with carbon quantum dots (C QDs). Finally, a possible photocatalytic reaction process is proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
0D/2D nanocomposite
Porous structure
Chemical scissor
Visible light
Photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples.
XPS survey spectra (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s for the as-prepared samples.
TEM images of (a) CN, (b) P-CN, (c) PCN and (d) C3N4 quantum dots (red dashed circle) on PCN.
AFM image (a) and corresponding cross-sectional profile (b) of typical PCN sample.
UV–vis DRS spectra of the as-prepared samples.

Photoelectrochemical properties of BCN and PCN. (a) The periodic on/off photocurrent performance of the as-prepared sample electrodes in 0.2 M Na2SO4 electrolyte with zero bias versus Ag/AgCl; (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots in the dark.
Photoluminescence spectra (a) and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra (b) of the samples under 400 nm excitation at 298 K.
The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples.
The photocatalytic activities of PCN modified by C quantum dots.
Stability of PCN-0.8% C QDs during prolonged photocatalytic operation.
The possible photocatalytic reaction process over PCN.
N 1s XPS fitting data of BCN, P-CN.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N1: CNC; N2: N(C)3; N3: CNH.

head.ce:title: Scalable one-pot synthesis of porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites for efficient visible-light driven photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Chemical Fertilizer Catalyst, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Chemical Fertilizer Catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-materials Advanced Technology (Fuzhou University), Fujian Province University, Fuzhou 350116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-materials Advanced Technology (Fuzhou University)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Minhou, Fujian 350116, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: National Engineering Research Center of Chemical Fertilizer Catalyst, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, China (Y. Zhan and S. Liang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Chemical Fertilizer Catalyst

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposite (PCN) has been successfully prepared by a simple and green one-pot photochemical oxidation method for the first time. Notably, the PCN samples are easily one-pot synthesized in hundreds of grams in the laboratory. The structure features, morphologies and photoabsorption performances of the samples are characterized in detail. The photocatalytic activities are evaluated by hydrogen evolution under visible light irradiation. It was proven that the green chemical scissor, produced by the photodecomposition of H2O2, can tailor the texture of layered C3N4 to form porous C3N4 nanosheets and C3N4 quantum dots (QDs) without doping heteroatom merely through the destruction of part of triazine rings, and the as-prepared PCN sample still keeps the basic chemical structure of g-C3N4. The band alignment between CN QDs and C3N4 nanosheets as well as the porous structure will facilitate the separation of charge carriers and provide more active sites. Therefore, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of PCN is over 30 times higher than that of the parent bulk g-C3N4. The activity is also higher than that of ultrathin pore-free C3N4 nanosheets and porous C3N4 nanosheets. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of PCN could be further improved by modifying with carbon quantum dots (C QDs). Finally, a possible photocatalytic reaction process is proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Since Wang and co-workers reported that graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) could produce hydrogen or oxygen by splitting water under visible light irradiation [1], g-C3N4-based photocatalysts have attracted increasing interest worldwide. Up to now, g-C3N4 photocatalysts have been used for water splitting into hydrogen, CO2 fixation, selective organic transformation and the decomposition of the pollutants [2–4]. However, the photocatalytic activity of bulk g-C3N4 is far from satisfactory due to poor electrical conductivity, the high exciton binding energy of photogenerated electron-hole pairs as well as few active sites and adsorption sites, etc [5–7].

To improve the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, many strategies were employed, such as morphology controlling, heteroatom doping, monomer engineering, coupling with other semiconductors and modification by carbonaceous nanomaterials [8–21]. In general, the electronic, optical and catalytic properties of g-C3N4 are deeply related with their nanoarchitectures. Therefore, many efforts have been devoted to tailor the texture of g-C3N4 materials [22–24], which can significantly increase the surface area and exposed active sites in g-C3N4, thus facilitating the mass transfer/transport of the reactant molecules, charge migration and separation, surface reactions and light harvesting. So far, many g-C3N4 with specific porous morphology have been widely fabricated by the template method [25–30], the solvent-assistant method [31,32], the post-thermal oxidation etching route [33–35], etc. Yet, to date, the search for a suitable, efficient and eco-friendly method for the preparation of porous C3N4 materials with high photocatalytic activity has remained a challenge. It is due to the fact that (1) tedious preparation procedures of template modification, precursor attachment and template removal limit the potential applications; (2) the highly corrosive chemical oxidants result in the environmentally-unfriendly wastes; (3) the photocatalysts prepared by the thermal oxidation and strong chemical oxidation methods contain various oxygen-containing functional groups and defect sites [36,37], leading to the consumption of the photogenerated electron/holes and the recombination of the charge carriers; (4) the nanoporous structure may not facilitate the mass transfer; (5) many reported methods give very small quantities of porous C3N4 nanosheets and are inherently un-scalable. It should also be noted that most of the reported papers focused on the synthesis of 3D porous g-C3N4 and few investigations concerning the synergistic effect between low-dimension porous structure and 0D/2D junction in C3N4 have been reported for photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Due to the low-dimension materials intriguing properties including the high surface area, short charge migration length and tunable electronic structure [38–40], lots of papers also reported to tune the dimensionality of g-C3N4 to improve its activity [41–43]. In our previous report, we have demonstrated that monolayer C3N4 nanosheets could be prepared by exfoliating layered g-C3N4 in the mixed solvents [44]. These nanosheets have the longer life time of charge carriers and rapider charge separation, and thus enhancing the photocatalytic activity of carbon nitride. These results could also be confirmed by other groups’ reports [41,42]. Besides ultrathin 2D nanosheet, 0D QDs [45,46] and 1D nanowires/nanorods/nanotubes [47–50] have been widely exploited and applied in the photocatalysis. Interestingly, due to the quantum confinement effects, the conduction band potentials of 2D nanosheet and 0D QDs of C3N4 are estimated to be about −0.95 V and −0.53 V vs. NHE (pH 0) [44,51,52] , which could form a band alignment. That is, the topology-induced band offset could engineer an isotype heterojunctions based on C3N4 without relying on extra materials. Thus, the obtained 0D/2D nanocomposites facilitate the separation of photogenerated charge carriers and the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity [7,53,54]. Considering the above advantages, it is expected that the nanocomposites consisted of porous 0D/2D C3N4 may be an excellent photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction.

Herein, a photochemical scissor has been developed to construct the ultrathin porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites (PCN) from the bulk g-C3N4 in one-pot and green approach for the first time. The structure features, morphologies and photoabsorption performances of the samples were characterized in detail. The photocatalytic activities were evaluated by hydrogen evolution under visible light irradiation. For comparison, nonporous and porous 2D C3N4 nanosheet without 0D C3N4 were also prepared as references. To further improve the photocatalytic activity of PCN, C QDs – a low-cost, earth-abundant and environmentally friendly materials as cocatalyst were also introduced onto the surface of PCN because C QDs could strongly interact with g-C3N4 and act as effective cocatalyst to catalyze the hydrogen evolution of g-C3N4 via a two-electron pathway [14,16]. Additionally, the photoelectric response and photoluminescence properties of PCN were also studied to evaluate the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Finally, a possible photocatalytic reaction process is proposed.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade agents and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. without further purification. Bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) was synthesized following the previous papers using melamine as precursor [1].

Porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites (PCN) were prepared by a photochemical oxidation method. In a typical procedure, 100 mg BCN was added into 100 mL deionized water containing 3 mL H2O2 (30%) in a quartz tube under vigorous stirring for 30 min. And then, the suspensions were irradiated by 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Perfectlight Co. Ltd.) for 2 h. Finally, the samples were collected by centrifugation and dried in a vacuum oven. It should be noted that the PCN samples are easily one-pot synthesized in hundreds of grams in the laboratory (Fig. S1). For comparison, the C3N4 nanosheets (CN) was prepared following our previous report [44], which was sonicated in pure water for 10 h and dried in a vacuum oven. Porous 2D C3N4 nanosheet (P-CN) without 0D C3N4 QDs was synthesized following the previous papers using melamine as precursor as well as H2SO4/HNO3 mixture solution as oxidant agent [36].
Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.07.164.

Porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites (PCN) were prepared by a photochemical oxidation method. In a typical procedure, 100 mg BCN was added into 100 mL deionized water containing 3 mL H2O2 (30%) in a quartz tube under vigorous stirring for 30 min. And then, the suspensions were irradiated by 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300, Beijing Perfectlight Co. Ltd.) for 2 h. Finally, the samples were collected by centrifugation and dried in a vacuum oven. It should be noted that the PCN samples are easily one-pot synthesized in hundreds of grams in the laboratory (Fig. S1 ). For comparison, the C3N4 nanosheets (CN) was prepared following our previous report [44], which was sonicated in pure water for 10 h and dried in a vacuum oven. Porous 2D C3N4 nanosheet (P-CN) without 0D C3N4 QDs was synthesized following the previous papers using melamine as precursor as well as H2SO4/HNO3 mixture solution as oxidant agent [36].

Carbon quantum dots (C QDs) were synthesized by using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as precursor. Generally, 0.5 g of EDTA was calcined in tube furnace at 400 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in N2 atmosphere. The product was purified by dispersing the carbon nanoparticles in acetone followed by centrifugation. The as-prepared C QDs with a concentration of 0.04 mg mL−1 were stored in acetone for use.

The PCN-C QDs composites were synthesized by an impregnation-precipitation method. Typically, C QDs acetone suspensions of a given volume and PCN of 0.1 g were added into ceramic crucible, heated at 90 °C under vigorous stirring until all the acetone boiled away. The as-prepared products were denoted as PCN-C QDs-x (x is the mass ratio of C QDs, x = 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, and 1.2%). BCN-C QDs, CN-C QDs and P-CN-C QDs composites were also prepared by a similar method except that the PCN were replaced by BCN, CN and P-CN.

The morphologies of the products were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI Nova NANO-SEM 230 spectrophotometer). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using a JEOL model JEM 2010 EX microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. A tapping-mode atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope Multimode IIIa, Veeco Instruments) with Si-tip cantilever was used to evaluate the morphology and thickness of the sample on the mica substrate. The crystal phases of the as-prepared samples were characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer operated at 40 kV and 40 mA with Ni-filtered Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of products were obtained on a Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer at a resolution of 4 cm−1. A total of 64 scans were performed to obtain each spectrum. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured with an ASAP2020M apparatus (Micromeritics Instrument Corp.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system with a monochromatic A1 Kα source and a charge neutralizer. All binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak (284.6 eV) of the surface adventitious carbon. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 500) and the data was converted by Kubelka-Munk (KM) functions. Barium sulfate was used as reference. The photoluminescence spectra were surveyed by an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer. The static photoluminescence (PL) spectra were carried out at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. For the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra, the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 375 nm and 460 nm, respectively.

The working electrode was prepared on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass, which was cleaned by sonicating in chloroform, acetone and ethanol for 30 min. The glass was then rinsed with pure water (18 MΩ cm) and dried in air. The FTO slide was dip coated with 10 μL of slurry, which was obtained from mixture of 5 mg powder and 0.5 mL dimethylformamide with sonicating for 2 h. After air drying naturally, a copper wire was connected to the side part of the FTO glass using a conductive tape. The uncoated parts of the electrode were isolated with an epoxy resin and the exposed area of the electrode was 0.25 cm2. The electrochemical measurements were performed in a conventional three electrode cell, using a Pt plate and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode were immersed in a 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution without any additive for 30 s before measurement. The photocurrent measurements were conducted with a CHI650E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China). A 300 W Xe lamp (Beijing Trustech, PLS-SXE300c) with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used as a light source. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out on electrochemical analyzer (Zahner IM6) was measured by applying 200 mV over the frequency ranged from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz in the dark.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution experiments were carried out in a Pyrex reaction cell connected to a closed gas circulation and evacuation system. 20 mg of photocatalysts were dispersed in 50 mL of aqueous solution containing 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEA) as a sacrificial agent and 1 wt% Pt as cocatalyst. The solution was irradiated by a 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm cut-off filter. The amount of hydrogen was measured by GC-8A gas chromatography (Shimadzu Co.) with TCD detector and a capillary column (molecular sieve 5 Å). High purity argon gas was used as a carrier gas.

The XRD patterns of BCN, P-CN and PCN have been shown in the Fig. 1 . For the BCN, two characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 which located at 13.6° and 27.4° could be observed. These peaks are derived from the in plane repeated units and the stacking of the conjugated aromatic systems in layered structure, respectively [55,56]. In contrast, the intensities of these two peaks over P-CN and PCN have decreased significantly after the photochemical oxidation process. That is, the periodic structure of C3N4 layers may be reduced [56].

FTIR spectra was further performed to characterize the chemical structure of the products. Fig. S2 shows the FTIR spectra of the as-prepared samples. The broad peaks between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 were originated from the NH and OH stretches [57]. It suggests that the partial hydrogenation of some nitrogen atoms in the as-prepared C3N4 samples. Clearly, the intensities of these peaks over P-CN and PCN are much higher than those of BCN, suggesting the higher amount of NH and OH species in P-CN and PCN. The peaks observed at 1317 and 1610 cm−1 were attributed to the C(sp2)N and C(sp2)N stretching vibration, respectively [58,59]. The peak at 807 cm−1 was related to breathing vibration of s-triazine ring system [60]. These characteristic peak positions in the FTIR spectra of P-CN and PCN are very similar to those of BCN, indicating that PCN keeps the basic chemical structure of C3N4. It should be noted that the structure features of P-CN are very similar with that of PCN.

FTIR spectra was further performed to characterize the chemical structure of the products. Fig. S2 shows the FTIR spectra of the as-prepared samples. The broad peaks between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 were originated from the NH and OH stretches [57]. It suggests that the partial hydrogenation of some nitrogen atoms in the as-prepared C3N4 samples. Clearly, the intensities of these peaks over P-CN and PCN are much higher than those of BCN, suggesting the higher amount of NH and OH species in P-CN and PCN. The peaks observed at 1317 and 1610 cm−1 were attributed to the C(sp2)N and C(sp2)N stretching vibration, respectively [58,59]. The peak at 807 cm−1 was related to breathing vibration of s-triazine ring system [60]. These characteristic peak positions in the FTIR spectra of P-CN and PCN are very similar to those of BCN, indicating that PCN keeps the basic chemical structure of C3N4. It should be noted that the structure features of P-CN are very similar with that of PCN.

The surface chemical states of the samples were characterized by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 . It could be clearly seen that only three elements (C, N, O) are detected in BCN, P-CN and PCN (Fig. 2a). P-CN and PCN exhibits C1s and N1s signals with a C/N molar ratio of 0.74, which is close to the parent BCN composition (C/N = 0.73) and the ideal g-C3N4 (C/N = 0.75). It is convinced that the chemical composition of carbon and nitrogen in P-CN and PCN are retained after the chemical oxidation process. Fig. 2b shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s. Both BCN, P-CN and PCN present obvious peaks at 284.6 and 287.9 eV, which are owing to the sp2 CC bonds and sp2-hybridized carbon in N-containing aromatic ring (NCN) [61]. The peak position and peak area of C 1s over PCN are the same as those of BCN and P-CN, indicating the similar coordination environment of carbon in among BCN, P-CN and PCN. No peak for C 1s of the CO bond could be observed, suggesting the absence of O doping in C3N4 layer. Clearly, the N 1s spectra can be also divided into three different peaks at binding energies of 398.2, 399.1 and 400.5 eV (Fig. 2c and Table 1 ), which are ascribed to the sp2-hybridized nitrogen involved in triazine ring (CNC, N1), the sp3-hybridized nitrogen N(C)3 groups (N2), and the free amino groups (CNH, N3), respectively [62,63]. Furthermore, the results indicate that the amount of free amino groups on BCN increases from 14.64% to 15.04% on PCN. A similar increasing tendency of sp3-hybridized nitrogen is also observed (from 23.59% to 24.31%). The decreased concentration of the sp2-hybridized nitrogen shows that some of triazine rings in g-C3N4 are destroyed through the photochemical oxidation process. However, the ratio of N1 is not change on P-CN and the ratio of N2 is decreased with increasing the ratio of N3. Thus, the porous structure on P-CN prepared by the oxidation process of H2SO4/HNO3 mixture solution may derived from the destruction of the heptazine rings in g-C3N4. For O 1s (Fig. 2d), the O 1s core level at 532.0 eV, ascribing to adsorbed hydroxyl species, as confirmed by the above FTIR analysis (Fig. S2). No additional signal related to the CO or NO bond is observed for both BCN, P-CN and PCN. That is, the photochemical oxidation process only destroys the texture of BCN and does not modify oxygen into the structure of C3N4 [64,65]. Furthermore, the peak area of O 1s over PCN is larger than that of BCN and P-CN, indicating PCN with higher amount of adsorbed hydroxyl species.

The surface chemical states of the samples were characterized by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 2 . It could be clearly seen that only three elements (C, N, O) are detected in BCN, P-CN and PCN (Fig. 2a). P-CN and PCN exhibits C1s and N1s signals with a C/N molar ratio of 0.74, which is close to the parent BCN composition (C/N = 0.73) and the ideal g-C3N4 (C/N = 0.75). It is convinced that the chemical composition of carbon and nitrogen in P-CN and PCN are retained after the chemical oxidation process. Fig. 2b shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s. Both BCN, P-CN and PCN present obvious peaks at 284.6 and 287.9 eV, which are owing to the sp2 CC bonds and sp2-hybridized carbon in N-containing aromatic ring (NCN) [61]. The peak position and peak area of C 1s over PCN are the same as those of BCN and P-CN, indicating the similar coordination environment of carbon in among BCN, P-CN and PCN. No peak for C 1s of the CO bond could be observed, suggesting the absence of O doping in C3N4 layer. Clearly, the N 1s spectra can be also divided into three different peaks at binding energies of 398.2, 399.1 and 400.5 eV (Fig. 2c and Table 1 ), which are ascribed to the sp2-hybridized nitrogen involved in triazine ring (CNC, N1), the sp3-hybridized nitrogen N(C)3 groups (N2), and the free amino groups (CNH, N3), respectively [62,63]. Furthermore, the results indicate that the amount of free amino groups on BCN increases from 14.64% to 15.04% on PCN. A similar increasing tendency of sp3-hybridized nitrogen is also observed (from 23.59% to 24.31%). The decreased concentration of the sp2-hybridized nitrogen shows that some of triazine rings in g-C3N4 are destroyed through the photochemical oxidation process. However, the ratio of N1 is not change on P-CN and the ratio of N2 is decreased with increasing the ratio of N3. Thus, the porous structure on P-CN prepared by the oxidation process of H2SO4/HNO3 mixture solution may derived from the destruction of the heptazine rings in g-C3N4. For O 1s (Fig. 2d), the O 1s core level at 532.0 eV, ascribing to adsorbed hydroxyl species, as confirmed by the above FTIR analysis (Fig. S2). No additional signal related to the CO or NO bond is observed for both BCN, P-CN and PCN. That is, the photochemical oxidation process only destroys the texture of BCN and does not modify oxygen into the structure of C3N4 [64,65]. Furthermore, the peak area of O 1s over PCN is larger than that of BCN and P-CN, indicating PCN with higher amount of adsorbed hydroxyl species.

The morphologies of the obtained products were investigated by SEM, TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. S3, it can be seen that the PCN prepared by the photochemical oxidation method displays a porous structure and irregular tissue-like 2D nanosheet morphology (Fig. S3a). Furthermore, the rough surface of PCN suggests some very small carbon nitride particles deposited on C3N4 nanosheets. However, the morphology of BCN exhibits a typical layered structure with a smooth surface. This could also be confirmed by TEM images. Fig. S4 shows that BCN exhibits a typical bulk structure. For the CN sample prepared by a liquid exfoliation, it has a laminar morphology with nearly transparent feature (Fig. 3 a), indicating its ultrathin thickness. In contrast, P-CN and PCN consist of lots of porous 2D nanosheets (Fig. 3b and c), which can be observed that the pore-sizes are within 20–40 nm and 5–15 nm, respectively. Compared with BCN, P-CN and PCN have are very thin structures. More interestingly, it is found that the C3N4 quantum dots (CN QDs) locate on the surface of PCN. The size of CN QDs is within 3–6 nm as shown in Fig. 3d. That is, the CN QDs modified porous C3N4 nanosheet composites could be directly prepared a simply photochemical oxidation process using BCN as precursor. The typical thickness and morphology of PCN nanosheet were further determined by AFM, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The as-prepared PCN nanosheet possesses porous structure, which is well consistent with the TEM and SEM results. Besides that, the cross-sectional AFM image shows that the thickness of the PCN nanosheet is about 0.9 nm (Fig. 4b). This thickness shows the PCN nanosheet may be composed by bilayer or trilayer C3N4.

The morphologies of the obtained products were investigated by SEM, TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. S3, it can be seen that the PCN prepared by the photochemical oxidation method displays a porous structure and irregular tissue-like 2D nanosheet morphology (Fig. S3a). Furthermore, the rough surface of PCN suggests some very small carbon nitride particles deposited on C3N4 nanosheets. However, the morphology of BCN exhibits a typical layered structure with a smooth surface. This could also be confirmed by TEM images. Fig. S4 shows that BCN exhibits a typical bulk structure. For the CN sample prepared by a liquid exfoliation, it has a laminar morphology with nearly transparent feature (Fig. 3 a), indicating its ultrathin thickness. In contrast, P-CN and PCN consist of lots of porous 2D nanosheets (Fig. 3b and c), which can be observed that the pore-sizes are within 20–40 nm and 5–15 nm, respectively. Compared with BCN, P-CN and PCN have are very thin structures. More interestingly, it is found that the C3N4 quantum dots (CN QDs) locate on the surface of PCN. The size of CN QDs is within 3–6 nm as shown in Fig. 3d. That is, the CN QDs modified porous C3N4 nanosheet composites could be directly prepared a simply photochemical oxidation process using BCN as precursor. The typical thickness and morphology of PCN nanosheet were further determined by AFM, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The as-prepared PCN nanosheet possesses porous structure, which is well consistent with the TEM and SEM results. Besides that, the cross-sectional AFM image shows that the thickness of the PCN nanosheet is about 0.9 nm (Fig. 4b). This thickness shows the PCN nanosheet may be composed by bilayer or trilayer C3N4.

The N2 sorption–desorption isotherms of BCN, P-CN and PCN are shown in Fig. S5. P-CN and PCN exhibit a Type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with H3 hysteresis loop in the relative pressure range of 0.5–1.0, indicating the samples with mesopore structure. It should be noted that the porosities of P-CN and PCN are much higher than that of BCN. This well agrees with the TEM and AFM results. The BET surface areas of P-CN and PCN are estimated about 60.3 and 62 m2 g−1, respectively, which are about 5 times larger than that of BCN (11.8 m2 g−1). The mean pore volume and mean pore size of PCN are about 0.543 cm3 g−1 and 39.4 nm, respectively, which are largely higher than 0.083 cm3 g−1 and 25.7 nm of BCN.

The N2 sorption–desorption isotherms of BCN, P-CN and PCN are shown in Fig. S5. P-CN and PCN exhibit a Type IV adsorption-desorption isotherms with H3 hysteresis loop in the relative pressure range of 0.5–1.0, indicating the samples with mesopore structure. It should be noted that the porosities of P-CN and PCN are much higher than that of BCN. This well agrees with the TEM and AFM results. The BET surface areas of P-CN and PCN are estimated about 60.3 and 62 m2 g−1, respectively, which are about 5 times larger than that of BCN (11.8 m2 g−1). The mean pore volume and mean pore size of PCN are about 0.543 cm3 g−1 and 39.4 nm, respectively, which are largely higher than 0.083 cm3 g−1 and 25.7 nm of BCN.

Based on the results of the aforementioned experiments, the formation mechanism of PCN may be represented as follows. Firstly, a part of bulk g-C3N4 are exfoliated into few-layered C3N4 under vigorous stirring due to the similar surface energy of H2O and g-C3N4. When the light is turned on, the H2O2 molecules would be decomposed into OH radicals. And then, the resultant OH radicals would attack the g-C3N4 to tailor the triazine rings in g-C3N4 to reduce the size of g-C3N4 and form the porous structure. Finally, the porous 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites are produced.

As the photoabsorption performances of the samples play a key role in determining the photocatalytic activity, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were recorded (Fig. 5 ). All of the samples have a strong absorption in the visible light region. Remarkably, the absorption edge of CN exhibits an obviously blue shift in comparison with that of BCN and a slightly blue shift is observed on P-CN and PCN due to the quantum size effects. The absorption edges of BCN, P-CN, PCN and CN are about 460, 455, 441 and 434 nm, respectively, corresponding to the optical bandgap values of 2.70, 2.72, 2.81 and 2.86 eV. The increased bandgap may improve the redox ability of photogenerated charges, which facilitates the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Furthermore, the photoabsorption performances of P-CN and PCN is slightly lower than that of BCN and CN in the range of 300–400 nm because of the porous structure and the reflection of the incident light with short wavelength on the 0D C3N4 nanodots.

In order to understand the charge transfer performances of as-prepared samples, the transient photocurrent responses of BCN, CN, P-CN and PCN were recorded for several on–off cycles of intermittent irradiation (Fig. 6 a). The photocurrent responds quickly in these two electrodes when the samples are exposed to the visible light irradiation. And then, the photocurrent value decreases to the initial value while the irradiation of light is off, and the photocurrent value comes back to a constant value as soon as the light is on again, which shows reproducible. The photocurrent value of PCN electrode is much higher than that of the BCN electrode and improved by a factor of 15. It is worth noting that PCN also shows an obviously enhanced photocurrent value compared with CN and P-CN. Furthermore, when the irradiation is switched off, the photocurrent value of the PCN sample decreases more slowly to the initial value than that of BCN, CN and P-CN. This indicates that many traps existing in PCN could more efficiently separate and store the photogenerated charges, reducing the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes [66]. EIS Nyquist plots were further performed to compare the charge transfer capability of the as-prepared samples. In Fig. 6b, the order of EIS arc sizes is r (BCN) > r (P-CN) > r (PCN) ≈ r (CN). The obviously reduced diameter of the semicircular Nyquist plots for PCN suggests that the charge transfer resistance of PCN is much lower than that of BCN and P-CN at the material surface/interface. That is, the photogenerated charge mobility and separation over PCN would be higher than that of BCN and P-CN, which is consistent with the tendency of photocurrent response.

To verify the above explanation, the photoluminescence (PL) analyses of the BCN, P-CN and PCN samples were performed. PL emission originates from the recombination of the photogenerated electrons and holes, so the stronger the PL peak intensity suggests the higher the recombination efficiency [67]. Fig. 7 a displays the PL spectra of the samples under excitation wavelength at 400 nm at room temperature. Both P-CN and PCN have a strong emission peak at 460 nm, while the peak of BCN locates at 470 nm. This shift may be due to the enlarged bandgaps of P-CN and PCN. Notably, these emission peak positions are very close to the photoabsorption edge of g-C3N4. It suggests that the π-conjugated system of C3N4 is not varied after the photochemical oxidation process [68]. PCN shows the lowest PL intensity compared with BCN and P-CN, indicating that PCN has a higher separation rate of the photogenerated holes and electrons.

In order to further understand the photophysical behaviors of photoexcited charge carriers, the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of BCN, P-CN and PCN were recorded (Fig. 7b). The fluorescent intensities of the samples decay exponentially. Clearly, in contrast to BCN and P-CN, PCN shows a much slower decay kinetics. That is, the life time of the charge carriers over PCN is longer than that of BCN and P-CN. In addition, according to the fitting calculation of the decay spectrum, the mean lifetime of the charge carriers on BCN, P-CN and PCN were 93.3, 100.9 and 114.4 ns, respectively (Table S1). It clearly confirmed the recombination of the photogenerated electron and hole is well suppressed in PCN.

In order to further understand the photophysical behaviors of photoexcited charge carriers, the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of BCN, P-CN and PCN were recorded (Fig. 7b). The fluorescent intensities of the samples decay exponentially. Clearly, in contrast to BCN and P-CN, PCN shows a much slower decay kinetics. That is, the life time of the charge carriers over PCN is longer than that of BCN and P-CN. In addition, according to the fitting calculation of the decay spectrum, the mean lifetime of the charge carriers on BCN, P-CN and PCN were 93.3, 100.9 and 114.4 ns, respectively (Table S1). It clearly confirmed the recombination of the photogenerated electron and hole is well suppressed in PCN.

Considering that PCN exhibits high separation rate of photogenerated charge carriers and strong redox ability of photogenerated charges, it may be supposed that PCN shows high photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution. Thus, the photocatalytic activities of the samples for the hydrogen evolution were evaluated under visible light (λ > 420 nm) irradiation by using triethanolamine as the sacrificial reagent to quench the photoinduced holes, and 1 wt% Pt as co-catalyst to reduce the overpotential for hydrogen evolution. As shown in Fig. 8 , all of the samples exhibit the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution. The H2 evolution rate of PCN is over 30, 2.4 and 1.6 times higher than that of BCN, P-CN and CN, respectively. Although the P-CN sample with the porous structure may provide numerous active sites, the sample prepared by a chemical oxidation with strong acids may also contain various oxygen-containing functional groups and abundant defect sites on the surface of the sample [36], leading to the consumption of the photogenerated electron/holes and facilitate the recombination of the charge carriers. As for CN, the ultrathin and the defect-free 2D structure make for the CN with high separation ratio of charge carriers. Therefore, P-CN represents a lower photocatalytic activity than that of CN.

To further improve the photocatalytic activity of PCN, carbon quantum dots (C QDs) are used as oxidation cocatalyst to modify on the surface of PCN by a simple impregnation-precipitation method [16]. Fig. 9 shows that the photocatalytic activity of PCN has been greatly improved after C QDs modified. The H2 evolution rates are increased with increasing the amount of C QDs from 0.4 wt% to 0.8 wt%. However, when the concentration of C QDs is higher than 0.8 wt%, the photocatalytic activity reduces. This is may be due to the fact that C QDs overload possibly shields the light absorption of PCN and becomes the recombination centre of photogenerated electrons and holes. Due to the fact that the introduction of C QDs may shadow the effect of 0D C3N4 QDs on the photocatalytic activity, we have evaluated the photocatalytic activities of other samples modified by C quantum dots of 0.8 wt%, as shown in Fig. S6. All of the samples exhibit an enhanced photocatalytic activity because the electrical conductivities of the samples are greatly improved (Fig. S7). It could be clearly seen that PCN-0.8% C QDs shows much higher photocatalytic activity than those of both CN-0.8% C QDs and P-CN-0.8% C QDs. Considering that the BET surface areas of both P-CN and PCN are very similar, the in-situ formation of 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites could more efficiently facilitate the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution through the enhanced separation of the photogenerated charge carrier. Notably, the porous structure could also promote the activity of C3N4. The stability of the photocatalytic activity of the PCN-C QDs sample has also been evaluated, as shown in Fig. 10 . The photocatalytic reactions were performed for a total of 20 h with evacuation every 4 h. The photocatalytic activity of the sample in the first cycle is lower than that of the second cycle because the Pt4+ cations is reduced into metallic Pt cocatalyst by a photodeposition method in the initial stage. After the induction period, it is clearly found that a continuous H2 evolution without obvious loss is observed during the reaction process. These results indicate the high-stability of the PCN sample.

To further improve the photocatalytic activity of PCN, carbon quantum dots (C QDs) are used as oxidation cocatalyst to modify on the surface of PCN by a simple impregnation-precipitation method [16]. Fig. 9 shows that the photocatalytic activity of PCN has been greatly improved after C QDs modified. The H2 evolution rates are increased with increasing the amount of C QDs from 0.4 wt% to 0.8 wt%. However, when the concentration of C QDs is higher than 0.8 wt%, the photocatalytic activity reduces. This is may be due to the fact that C QDs overload possibly shields the light absorption of PCN and becomes the recombination centre of photogenerated electrons and holes. Due to the fact that the introduction of C QDs may shadow the effect of 0D C3N4 QDs on the photocatalytic activity, we have evaluated the photocatalytic activities of other samples modified by C quantum dots of 0.8 wt%, as shown in Fig. S6. All of the samples exhibit an enhanced photocatalytic activity because the electrical conductivities of the samples are greatly improved (Fig. S7). It could be clearly seen that PCN-0.8% C QDs shows much higher photocatalytic activity than those of both CN-0.8% C QDs and P-CN-0.8% C QDs. Considering that the BET surface areas of both P-CN and PCN are very similar, the in-situ formation of 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposites could more efficiently facilitate the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution through the enhanced separation of the photogenerated charge carrier. Notably, the porous structure could also promote the activity of C3N4. The stability of the photocatalytic activity of the PCN-C QDs sample has also been evaluated, as shown in Fig. 10 . The photocatalytic reactions were performed for a total of 20 h with evacuation every 4 h. The photocatalytic activity of the sample in the first cycle is lower than that of the second cycle because the Pt4+ cations is reduced into metallic Pt cocatalyst by a photodeposition method in the initial stage. After the induction period, it is clearly found that a continuous H2 evolution without obvious loss is observed during the reaction process. These results indicate the high-stability of the PCN sample.

Based on the results of the aforementioned experiments, the high performances of PCN-C QDs may be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the porous structure, the band alignment between CN QDs and C3N4 nanosheets, and the boosting effect of cocatalyst. Therefore, we propose a tentative mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the PCN-C QDs (Scheme 1 ). Both CN QDs and g-C3N4 nanosheets can absorb visible light to produce photoinduced electron-hole pairs. According to the band structure, the conduction band and valance band levels of 2D nanosheets are more negative than those of 0D QDs, as shown in Scheme 1. It is beneficial to the photogenerated electrons transfer from the CB of 2D nanosheets to that of 0D QDs. The electrons accumulate on the surface of 0D QDs and the deposited Pt. Meanwhile, C QDs may contribute to the hydrogen evolution of C3N4 via a two-electron pathway. The photogenerated holes on 0D QDs directly transfer to 2D nanosheets and C QDs. And then, the holes and the other possible oxidation products (H2O2, etc) are consumed by the sacrificial agent TEA. It is worthy note that nanosheets with porous structure would provide more reactive site for the hydrogen evolution and may reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Therefore, an effective charge separation can be achieved, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity and stability of the photocatalyst.

In summary, a 0D/2D C3N4 nanocomposite (PCN) has been successfully prepared by a simple and green one-pot photochemical oxidation method for the first time. The as-prepared PCN still retains the structural features of C3N4. In addition, the porous composite endows the novel electronic structure and distinctive physicochemical properties, including high separation rate of photogenerated charge carriers and high percentage of exposed active sites. Therefore, the as-prepared PCN shows the superior photocatalytic activities for hydrogen production under visible light irradiation compared with the parent bulk g-C3N4 (BCN), ultrathin pore-free C3N4 nanosheets and porous C3N4 nanosheets. The H2 production amount of PCN is over 30 times higher than that of BCN. It should be noted that the photocatalytic activities of PCN could be further improved after modified cocatalyst C QDs. The photochemical oxidation method is easy and efficient to reduce the dimension of the layered carbon nitride without doping heteroatom. Thus, it may be expected that this method will provide a similar chemical scissor to tailor the soft matters.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21677036), the Natural Science Funds for Distinguished Young Scholar of Fujian Province (2016 J06004) and Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2016J01057).
